lessons.title: JDBC Basics
lessons.summary: Learn JDBC architecture and driver types, connecting with DriverManager.getConnection(), and the basic usage of Connection, Statement, and ResultSet.

contents.title: What Is JDBC?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- JDBC architecture (driver → connection → SQL execution → result retrieval)
- How to obtain a DB connection with DriverManager
- The structure and meaning of connection URLs

This covers the foundations of Java's standard API for accessing databases.

contents.title: JDBC Architecture and Drivers
contents.body:
The JDBC architecture consists of four layers: the application, JDBC Driver Manager, JDBC driver, and database.
The **JDBC** URL is a string specifying the database connection target, in the format jdbc:subprotocol:subname.
Examples: jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/mydb, jdbc:postgresql://localhost/testdb
The DriverManager class handles driver management and connection establishment.
Since [[JDBC]] 4.0, drivers are automatically detected through service provider configuration under META-INF/services.
Explicit driver loading with Class.forName() is no longer required, but it may be tested as exam knowledge.
contents.note: The **JDBC** URL format varies by database vendor. The basic URL format is tested on the exam.

contents.title: Connecting to a Database
contents.body:
DriverManager.getConnection() connects to a database.
You specify the JDBC URL, username, and password as arguments.
The return value is a Connection object, which represents the database connection.
Connection implements [[AutoCloseable]], so it can be used with [[try-with-resources]].
If the connection fails, a SQLException is thrown.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Statement and ResultSet
contents.body:
A Statement is an object for executing SQL, obtained with Connection.createStatement().
executeQuery() executes a SELECT statement and returns a [[ResultSet]].
executeUpdate() executes INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE and returns the number of affected rows.
ResultSet is a cursor over the query results; next() moves to the next row.
When next() returns false, there are no more results. Use getString(), getInt(), etc. to retrieve column values.
contents.note: ResultSet column indices are 1-based. Specifying 0 will throw a SQLException.

contents.title: Exam Prep: JDBC Basics
contents.body:
Memorize the basic format of **JDBC** URLs (jdbc:subprotocol:subname).
Connection, Statement, and ResultSet all implement AutoCloseable.
executeQuery() returns a ResultSet, while executeUpdate() returns an int.
ResultSet column indices are 1-based, and values can be retrieved by either column name or column index.
The closing order for resources is ResultSet → Statement → Connection.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is the correct basic format of a JDBC URL?
quizzes.explanation: The basic format of a JDBC URL is jdbc:subprotocol:subname. The subprotocol specifies the database type (mysql, postgresql, etc.), and the subname contains detailed connection information.

quizzes.question: What is the return type of Statement.executeQuery()?
quizzes.explanation: executeQuery() executes a SELECT statement and returns the results as a ResultSet. executeUpdate() executes INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements and returns the number of affected rows as an int.

quizzes.question: ResultSet column indices are ___-based, and specifying 0 throws a SQLException.
quizzes.explanation: ResultSet column indices are 1-based. getString(1) retrieves the value of the first column. Specifying 0 throws a SQLException. Note that this differs from array indices, which are 0-based.

lessons.title: Executing SQL
lessons.summary: Learn in detail how to execute SELECT statements with Statement and retrieve results with ResultSet, execute INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements, and use ResultSet navigation methods.

contents.title: SQL Execution Overview
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to execute SQL with Statement
- The difference between executeQuery() (SELECT) and executeUpdate() (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
- How to retrieve results from a [[ResultSet]]

You will learn the basic flow of SQL execution.

contents.title: SELECT with executeQuery()
contents.body:
executeQuery() executes a SELECT statement and returns a ResultSet.
The next() [[method]] of ResultSet moves the cursor to the next row.
It returns true if there is data and false if there is none, so you can process all rows with a while loop.
There are type-specific retrieval methods such as getString(), getInt(), and getDouble().
Column values are retrieved by column name or column index (1-based).
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: DML Operations with executeUpdate()
contents.body:
executeUpdate() executes INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statements and returns the number of affected rows.
It can also be used for DDL (CREATE TABLE, DROP TABLE, etc.), in which case it returns 0.
The execute() method works with all SQL statements and returns true if the result is a ResultSet, or false if it is an update count.
Use getResultSet() or getUpdateCount() to retrieve the results.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: ResultSet Navigation
contents.body:
The default ResultSet is TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY, which only allows forward movement.
To create a scrollable ResultSet, specify arguments in createStatement().
| Constant | Description |
|---|---|
| TYPE_SCROLL_INSENSITIVE | Allows forward and backward movement but does not reflect database changes |
| TYPE_SCROLL_SENSITIVE | Allows forward and backward movement and reflects database changes |
| CONCUR_READ_ONLY | Read-only. CONCUR_UPDATABLE allows updates |
contents.note: Calling first() or previous() on a TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY ResultSet throws a SQLException.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Executing SQL
contents.body:
Passing a non-SELECT statement to executeQuery() throws a SQLException.
Passing a SELECT statement to executeUpdate() also throws a SQLException.
execute() works with all SQL statements and determines the result type as a boolean.
The ResultSet cursor is initially positioned before the first row.
Memorize the TYPE and CONCUR combinations for scrollable ResultSets.

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the Statement.execute() method?
quizzes.explanation: The execute() method can execute all types of SQL statements. It returns true if the result is a ResultSet (SELECT), and false if it is an update count (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DDL).

choices.text: Can execute all SQL statements; returns true for ResultSet, false for update count
Can only execute SELECT statements and returns a ResultSet
Can only execute UPDATE statements and returns the update count
Can execute all SQL statements and always returns a ResultSet

quizzes.question: If the products table has two rows: {id=1, name="Pen", price=100} and {id=2, name="Book", price=500}, what is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: The UPDATE statement updates rows matching the condition price < 300. The Pen with price=100 matches, while the Book with price=500 does not. Therefore, 1 row is updated and executeUpdate() returns 1.

quizzes.question: The default ResultSet type is TYPE___ONLY, which only allows forward cursor movement.
quizzes.explanation: The default ResultSet type is TYPE_FORWARD_ONLY. With this type, only next() can be used to move the cursor forward. Reverse operations such as previous() and first() throw a SQLException.

quizzes.question: Complete the code that sets parameters with PreparedStatement.
quizzes.explanation: prepareStatement() prepares the SQL, and setInt() sets a value for the placeholder (?). This prevents SQL injection.
quizzes.codeTemplate:
PreparedStatement ps = conn.__BLANK_1__("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?");
ps.__BLANK_2__(1, userId);

lessons.title: PreparedStatement
lessons.summary: Learn how to use PreparedStatement for parameterized queries, set parameters with setString() and setInt(), and understand how SQL injection is prevented.

contents.title: What Is PreparedStatement?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The advantages of **PreparedStatement**
- How to use parameter placeholders (?)
- Preventing **SQL injection** attacks

This is a SQL execution method that excels in both security and performance.

contents.title: Using Parameterized Queries
contents.body:
In [[PreparedStatement]], the ? in the SQL statement serves as a placeholder.
You bind values to parameters with setString(), setInt(), setDouble(), setDate(), etc.
Parameter indices are 1-based.
After binding, execute with executeQuery() or executeUpdate().
The same PreparedStatement object can be reused with different parameters.
contents.note: Parameter indices are 1-based. Specifying 0 throws a SQLException.

contents.title: Various set Methods
contents.body:
PreparedStatement provides type-specific set methods.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| setString(int, String) | Sets a string-type parameter. |
| setInt(int, int) | Sets an integer-type parameter. |
| setDouble(int, double) | Sets a floating-point parameter. |
| setDate(int, java.sql.Date) | Sets a date-type parameter. |
| setNull(int, int) | Sets a NULL value. The second argument is a java.sql.Types constant. |
| setObject(int, Object) | Sets an object generically. |

contents.title: Preventing SQL Injection
contents.body:
**SQL injection** is an attack technique that tampers with SQL statements through malicious input.
Using string concatenation with Statement is dangerous when input contains SQL syntax.
PreparedStatement prevents SQL injection because parameter values are not interpreted as SQL syntax.
By using ? placeholders, the supplied values are always treated as data.
From a security perspective, SQL that includes user input should always use PreparedStatement.
contents.note: Never build SQL with string concatenation. Always use PreparedStatement placeholders.

contents.title: Exam Prep: PreparedStatement
contents.body:
PreparedStatement parameter indices are 1-based.
Memorize the correspondence between the types set with setXxx() and the database column types.
PreparedStatement can be reused — reset parameters and execute multiple times.
Passing a SQL string to executeQuery() throws a SQLException (use the no-argument version, not the one [[inherited]] from Statement).
The mechanism of **SQL injection** prevention may be tested on the exam.

quizzes.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of using PreparedStatement?
quizzes.explanation: The benefits of PreparedStatement are SQL injection prevention and performance improvement. It does not provide automatic transaction management. Transaction management is handled by the Connection object.

choices.text: It can prevent SQL injection attacks
The SQL is precompiled for improved performance
Parameters can be bound to safely execute SQL statements

quizzes.question: In a PreparedStatement SQL string, the symbol that indicates a parameter position is ___.
quizzes.explanation: In PreparedStatement, the ? (question mark) in the SQL statement functions as a placeholder. Values are bound to the ? positions using methods like setString() and setInt().

quizzes.question: The following code has an error. Which part is problematic?
quizzes.explanation: PreparedStatement parameter indices are 1-based. setInt(0, 42) specifies the invalid index 0, which causes a SQLException. The correct call is setInt(1, 42).

choices.text: The SQL in prepareStatement() is invalid

quizzes.question: Select all major JDBC interfaces.
quizzes.explanation: The major JDBC interfaces are Connection, Statement, ResultSet, and DataSource. There is no interface called Database.

lessons.title: Transactions
lessons.summary: Learn JDBC transaction management. Understand starting transactions with setAutoCommit(false), using commit() and rollback(), Savepoints, and the basics of DataSource.

contents.title: Transaction Management Overview
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The concept of **transactions** (ACID properties)
- How to use setAutoCommit(false), commit(), and rollback()
- Combining [[transactions]] with [[try-with-resources]]

This is an essential mechanism for maintaining data integrity.

contents.title: commit() and rollback()
contents.body:
In manual **transaction** management, you first call setAutoCommit(false).
If a series of SQL operations succeeds, call commit() to finalize; if it fails, call rollback() to undo.
Calling rollback() undoes all changes since the last commit().
A try-catch-[[finally]] pattern is commonly used to ensure rollback() on error.
After processing, it is recommended to reset to setAutoCommit(true).
contents.note: Without calling setAutoCommit(false), each SQL statement is automatically committed individually.

contents.title: Savepoint
contents.body:
A Savepoint is an intermediate checkpoint that can be set within a **transaction**.
[[Connection]].setSavepoint() creates a Savepoint.
rollback(Savepoint) allows you to partially roll back to a specific Savepoint.
Rolling back to a Savepoint does not end the transaction itself.
releaseSavepoint() releases a Savepoint.
A named Savepoint is created with setSavepoint([[String]] name).
contents.note: After rolling back to a Savepoint, the **transaction** continues. A final commit() or rollback() is still required.

contents.title: DataSource Overview
contents.body:
DataSource is an alternative means of obtaining JDBC connections, defined by the javax.sql.DataSource interface.
Compared to DriverManager, its major advantage is support for connection pooling (connection reuse).
Connection pooling reduces the overhead of creating and destroying connections.
Using DataSource is recommended in production applications.
It is common to obtain a DataSource through JNDI (Java Naming and Directory Interface).
The concept and advantages of DataSource are tested on the exam.
contents.note: With DataSource, close() does not physically disconnect but returns the connection to the pool.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Transactions
contents.body:
The default is AutoCommit mode (true), where each SQL statement is automatically committed.
Without setAutoCommit(false), commit()/rollback() have no effect.
rolling back to a Savepoint does not end the **transaction** — this is an important point.
Connection pooling is a key feature of DataSource.
Memorize the four elements of ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability).

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the default transaction behavior in JDBC?
quizzes.explanation: By default in JDBC, AutoCommit mode is enabled (true). Each SQL statement is automatically committed upon execution. To manage transactions manually, you need to call setAutoCommit(false).

choices.text: AutoCommit is enabled, and each SQL statement is automatically committed
AutoCommit is disabled, and commit() must be called explicitly
Transactions are always managed with Savepoints
The transaction mode must be selected at connection time

quizzes.question: Which statement about Savepoint is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Calling rollback(Savepoint) undoes changes up to the specified Savepoint. However, the transaction itself does not end. You can continue with more SQL operations and ultimately end the transaction with commit() or rollback().

choices.text: Rolling back to a Savepoint does not end the transaction
Rolling back to a Savepoint automatically commits the transaction
A Savepoint can be set outside of a transaction
Only one Savepoint can be set per transaction

quizzes.question: What is the correct output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: JDBC connections have AutoCommit set to true by default. getAutoCommit() returns true. After calling setAutoCommit(false) to disable it, getAutoCommit() returns false.
